questions.question:
Which one of the following sets of pieces can be assembled to form a square with a single round hole near the center? Pieces cannot overlap.

A. [Four irregular interlocking pieces with a circular cutout among them]
B. [Four irregular pieces that do not combine into a complete square]
C. [Four irregular interlocking pieces that fit together to form a perfect square with a round hole at center]
D. [Four pieces with mismatched edges that leave gaps when assembled]
questions.answer: C. The set of pieces in option C assembles into a square with a single round hole near the center
questions.to_find: Which set of pieces assembles into a square with a single round hole near the center

understand.plain: You are given four options, each containing a set of irregular flat pieces. You must mentally rotate, flip, and fit the pieces together to see which group forms a complete square shape with exactly one circular hole positioned near its center. Pieces in the correct option will interlock perfectly without gaps or overlaps.

keywords.explain: Mentally arranging given flat shapes so their edges fit together to form a specified target shape
keywords.example: Fitting four L-shaped tiles together to form a 2x2 square

keywords.explain: The ability to mentally rotate, flip, or translate 2D shapes and predict how they combine
keywords.example: Recognizing that a piece rotated 90 degrees fills a gap in a partially assembled shape

keywords.explain: One of the pieces contains a circular cutout; when assembled the hole appears near the middle of the completed square
keywords.example: A jigsaw piece with a semicircular notch pairs with another piece that has a matching semicircle to form a full circle at the join

understand.visual_alt: A square divided into four interlocking pieces with a circular hole near the center, illustrating the target assembled shape

given.aim: Identify which option's pieces fit together without overlap to form a square with one round hole near the center

terms.meaning: A square with a single circular (round) hole positioned near its center
terms.example: Think of a square tile with a circular drain hole in the middle
terms.connects: All pieces combined must exactly reproduce this outline and hole

terms.meaning: Each piece occupies a unique region; no two pieces share any area
terms.example: Like fitting jigsaw puzzle pieces — each slot is filled by exactly one piece
terms.connects: Eliminates options where pieces double up or leave voids

terms.meaning: Each piece may be translated (moved), rotated, and/or flipped before placing
terms.example: A staircase-shaped piece rotated 180 degrees may fit a different corner of the square
terms.connects: Expands the ways pieces can interlock, so all orientations must be considered

terms.meaning: One piece (or the join between two pieces) contains a semicircular or full circular void that becomes the hole
terms.example: Two pieces each with a quarter-circle notch, placed adjacently, form a semicircle at the join
terms.connects: Confirms the assembled result has the required round hole at center

given.plan: For each option, mentally rotate and flip each piece and attempt to tile them inside a square boundary. Check whether pieces cover the entire square area exactly once, and whether the round hole falls near the center. Option C is the one where all pieces mesh perfectly with the hole centered.

steps.title: Identify the target outline
steps.apply: The target is a square. Count the total area covered by the pieces in each option. The piece set whose total area equals the square area minus the circular hole area is a candidate.
steps.note: Area consistency is a quick elimination check before detailed spatial reasoning.

steps.title: Test options A, B, D by edge matching
steps.apply: Options A, B, and D each contain pieces whose concave and convex edges do not complement each other when arranged inside a square. Attempting assembly reveals either gaps along the boundary or interior voids that are not the required circular hole.
steps.note: Mismatched step-edges or extra notches that cannot pair up quickly rule out incorrect options.

steps.title: Verify option C assembles correctly
steps.apply: In option C, the four pieces have complementary step-cuts and curved edges. Placing the piece containing the semicircular notch adjacent to its matching piece creates the full round hole. The remaining two pieces fill the outer corners. The assembled outline is a complete square with the hole near center.
steps.note: The circular hole is formed by two pieces each contributing a semicircular cutout meeting at the interior join.

solution.result: Option C is the correct answer. The pieces in set C can be assembled to form a square with a single round hole near the center.

questions.formula_note: This spatial aptitude question requires visual reasoning rather than mathematical formulas; no formula registry entries apply.

questions.question:
A jigsaw puzzle has 2 pieces. One of the pieces is shown above. Which one of the given options for the missing piece when assembled will form a rectangle? The piece can be moved, rotated or flipped to assemble with the above piece.

A. [A piece with interlocking notches that is the mirror complement of the given piece]
B. [A piece with a different notch pattern that does not complement the given piece]
C. [A piece with extra protrusions that overlap the given piece]
D. [A piece whose straight edge does not match the step-cut profile of the given piece]
questions.answer: A. The piece shown in option A is the missing piece that completes the rectangle
questions.to_find: Which missing piece, when assembled with the given piece, forms a complete rectangle

understand.plain: A rectangular jigsaw puzzle is cut into exactly two pieces. One piece is shown — it has an irregular stepped or notched boundary along the interior cut. You must find the option whose piece, after possibly rotating or flipping it, has a boundary that is the exact complement of the shown piece so that together they fill a rectangle with no gaps or overlaps.

keywords.explain: A piece whose cut profile is the exact negative (mirror) of another piece's cut profile so they interlock perfectly
keywords.example: If one piece has a rectangular tab sticking out, its complement has a matching rectangular slot

keywords.explain: When the two pieces are assembled the outer boundary must be a rectangle — all right-angle corners and straight sides
keywords.example: Two L-shaped pieces placed together can form a 2x3 rectangle

keywords.explain: The missing piece may be turned to any angle (0, 90, 180, 270 degrees) or reflected before matching
keywords.example: A piece that looks wrong right-side-up may fit perfectly when flipped horizontally

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 340 140' width='340' height='140'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='140' height='100' rx='4' class='bg'/><polygon points='10,20 80,20 80,50 110,50 110,20 150,20 150,120 10,120' class='hi'/><polygon points='80,20 110,20 110,50 80,50' fill='white' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='80' y='136' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>Given piece</text><polygon points='185,20 215,20 215,50 245,50 245,20 310,20 310,120 185,120' class='bg'/><polygon points='215,20 245,20 245,50 215,50' class='hi'/><text x='248' y='136' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>Missing piece (A)</text><text x='170' y='75' class='big ink' text-anchor='middle'>+</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The given stepped piece on the left and its complementary missing piece on the right, showing how they interlock to form a rectangle

given.aim: Find the piece that completes the 2-piece jigsaw to form a perfect rectangle when the piece can be moved, rotated, or flipped

terms.meaning: The irregular stepped outline of the piece already shown — its notches and tabs define exactly what the missing piece must look like
terms.example: A staircase-profile cut along the interior means the missing piece must have the inverse staircase
terms.connects: Every protrusion on the given piece corresponds to a recess on the missing piece and vice versa

terms.meaning: The total shape formed by both pieces is a rectangle, so the outer boundary has only straight sides and right-angle corners
terms.example: A 4x6 rectangle cut into two pieces — reassembled pieces reproduce the same 4x6 boundary
terms.connects: Ensures that non-rectangular outer edges on any option immediately disqualify it

terms.meaning: The missing piece may be placed in any position, any rotation (multiple of 90 deg), and either face up or flipped
terms.example: Option A when rotated 180 degrees aligns its step-cuts perfectly with the given piece
terms.connects: Without testing all orientations, a correct piece might be incorrectly rejected

terms.meaning: The shared boundary between the two pieces — both pieces must have identical (matching) cut lines for the assembly to work
terms.example: If the cut has three rectangular notches, the missing piece must have three matching tabs of the same dimensions
terms.connects: Matching the interior cut line is the primary verification step

given.plan: Trace the interior cut profile of the given piece. For each option, check whether its cut profile — after any rotation or flip — is the exact complement (negative shape) of the given piece's cut. The correct option will have a complementary profile and together with the given piece will produce a straight rectangular outer boundary.

steps.title: Trace the interior cut of the given piece
steps.apply: The given piece has a stepped notch pattern along its right edge — a series of rectangular tabs and slots running top-to-bottom. Count and note the dimensions of each tab and slot.
steps.note: Careful observation of the step sequence (tab-slot-tab or slot-tab-slot) is critical for matching.

steps.title: Check outer boundary of each option
steps.apply: Options B, C, and D have outer edges that are not straight or have incorrect overall proportions. When mentally combined with the given piece, the outer boundary does not form a clean rectangle, eliminating them.
steps.note: Any option whose outer silhouette (ignoring the interior cut) does not match the corresponding half of the target rectangle is eliminated.

steps.title: Verify option A by profile matching
steps.apply: Option A, when rotated 180 degrees (or flipped), presents an interior cut profile that is the exact negative of the given piece's profile — every tab on one piece fits into the corresponding slot on the other. The assembled outer boundary is a clean rectangle.
steps.note: The fit is confirmed when all concave indentations of one piece are exactly filled by the convex protrusions of the other with no overlap and no gap.

solution.result: Option A is the correct answer. The piece in option A, when assembled with the given piece (allowing rotation or flipping), forms a complete rectangle.

questions.formula_note: This spatial aptitude question is purely visual; no mathematical formulas from the registry are applicable.
